title: Quick check, following a call across B2BUAs

questions.prompt: A capture shows two SIP dialogs with different Call-IDs, each started within 30 milliseconds of the other on the same SBC. What does this almost certainly indicate?
choices: Two unrelated calls that happened to be placed at the same time
A B2BUA terminating one dialog and originating another for the same logical call
questions.explanation: B2BUAs act as a UAS to the originator and a UAC to the destination, so they terminate the inbound dialog and start a fresh outbound one with its own Call-ID and tags. Two dialogs starting within milliseconds of each other on the same SBC is the visible fingerprint of that bridging.

questions.prompt: You captured only on the customer-facing side of the PBX and the dialog ends with a 4xx the customer didn't initiate. What's the most useful next move?
choices: Re-run the customer-facing capture for longer
Add a second capture point on the carrier-facing side (or PBX pcap) for the same time window to see who upstream said no
Assume the carrier is at fault and escalate
Inspect the customer endpoint's local audio settings
questions.explanation: The customer-facing capture only shows the leg the customer sees. A B2BUA translates upstream failures into customer-facing responses without preserving the original reason. Capturing on the carrier-facing leg (PBX pcap, trunk SBC mirror) for the same time window reveals which leg actually rejected the call.
